coredata.dc:description: Internal short circuits constitute a significant risk to the safety and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs). Internal short circuits are among the most problematic failure mechanisms in LiBs because of their ability to cause thermal runaway and catastrophic failure and inherent difficulty of detection. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the types of internal short circuits, their origins, and the experimental methods used to reproduce them. Firstly, the definition and classification of internal short circuits, as well as their associated consequences from minor performance loss to thermal runaway, are described. Secondly, the root causes of internal short circuits are investigated, including a thorough examination of each manufacturing stage where defects can appear, focusing on preventive measures. Finally, the review details experimental approaches to reproduce internal short circuits mentioning their respective advantages and drawbacks with a focus on mechanical internal short circuit reproduction devices, such as nail/needle penetration techniques. These techniques are the easiest to implement but have an important number of variable parameters that can influence the test outcome.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Internal short circuits constitute a significant risk to the safety and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs). Internal short circuits are among the most problematic failure mechanisms in LiBs because of their ability to cause thermal runaway and catastrophic failure and inherent difficulty of detection. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the types of internal short circuits, their origins, and the experimental methods used to reproduce them. Firstly, the definition and classification of internal short circuits, as well as their associated consequences from minor performance loss to thermal runaway, are described. Secondly, the root causes of internal short circuits are investigated, including a thorough examination of each manufacturing stage where defects can appear, focusing on preventive measures. Finally, the review details experimental approaches to reproduce internal short circuits mentioning their respective advantages and drawbacks with a focus on mechanical internal short circuit reproduction devices, such as nail/needle penetration techniques. These techniques are the easiest to implement but have an important number of variable parameters that can influence the test outcome.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: According to Global EV forecast, Electric vehicle production could represent up to 75 % of passenger car production in Europe by 2030 depending on the type of vehicle (light duty, HEV,SUV, truck, buses.) [1]. To optimize the performance and range of electric vehicles, Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEM) have to increase the specific energy of the pack. One way is to use larger cells containing more energy, which leads to a more energy-dense design once integrated into the module and pack. This could make safety issues even more crucial to consider since the thermal runaway of a single cell would likely become a more critical event. Battery thermal runaway is a well-known hazardous phenomenon, characterized by chain reactions inducing exothermic reactions that generate heat inside the battery faster than heat dissipation, leading to a fast rise in battery temperature [2–9]. Thermal runaway can be triggered by several factors, including overcharging, overvoltage, external short circuiting, overheating or internal short circuits. In the case of an internal short circuit, battery thermal runaway can occur quickly (from few seconds to tens of minutes and more) and unexpectedly, making it difficult to predict [2–9]. Internal short circuits in batteries can be induced by various causes, including manufacturing defects [10,11], mechanical damage [11–18], incorrect interconnection, repeated over-discharging [11,19–24], or overcharging [11,21–23,25–36]. When an internal short circuit occurs in a cell, in the best case, it will result in a slow discharge, increasing the self-discharge and consequently imbalance of the State Of Charge (SOC) among the cells in the pack. Alternatively, it may cause overheating, which in the worst case leads to thermal runaway (TR) of the cell. Many internal short circuit tests have been developed to simulate such event. However, these efforts have not yet led to a clear consensus on an internal short circuit representative test method [37].

Recent advances in internal short circuit research for lithium-ion batteries have each contributed important but distinct perspectives. Orendorff et al. [38] introduced an internal short circuit initiation via low-melting-point metals, focusing on small formats, though reliability in standard cells like 18650s needs improvement. Spotnitz et al. [30] modeled abuse behavior and thermal runaway but presented limited amount of experimental data and did not discriminate internal short circuit types. The work of Zhang et al. [39] focus on methods in order to replicate internal short circuits. It details particularly the shape memory alloys method and gives only few elements on other triggering methods.

This review integrates theses perspectives and complement them starting with a description of internal short circuits types and consequences as reported in literature. Then, their root causes are detailed, with a focus on manufacturing defects and Li dendrite growth over ageing. Techniques so far developed for the early detection of internal short circuits are also presented. Finally, the experimental setups used to mimic an internal short circuit or replicate a cell's response to an internal short circuit are examined and their subsequent limitations discussed. In the following, we chose to use the conventional terms 'anode' and 'cathode' when describing the battery electrodes, though we acknowledge this constitutes a technical misnomer for rechargeable systems. Strictly speaking, these should be designated as the 'negative electrode' and 'positive electrode' respectively, since their redox roles reverse between charge and discharge cycles. This terminology choice follows common practice in battery literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal short circuit types and consequences
ce:section.ce:para: Depending on the root cause, it has been shown in the literature that an internal short circuit typology can be sorted in different categories [19,39–48] as presented in Fig. 1 depending in the elements in contact: i) contact between the two current collectors (copper and aluminum), ii) contact between the copper current collector and cathode active material, iii) contact between aluminum current collector and anode material, iv) contact between the active materials of the two electrodes. The actual consequences of an internal short circuit may significantly vary depending on various parameters such as the geometrical and electrochemical characteristics of the cells (e.g., format, capacity, voltage, SOC, …) and its features (e.g., type, size, location and resistance): They may range from a minor failure (capacity loss …) to the thermal runaway of the cell [19,39–48]. A crucial internal short circuit characteristic is the short-circuit resistance; as developed by Lai et al. [19], Volck et al. [46], Lui et al. [41] and Orendorff et al. [38], short-circuits can be classified in different type according to their Ohmic resistance. Two categories are usually distinguished: Soft internal short circuit and Hard internal short circuit [19,38,41,49].

It is important to note that the concept of an internal short circuit varies slightly among authors. While most researchers refer to the same underlying phenomenon, they often employ different terminologies and criteria to describe it. Some definitions emphasize the recoverability of voltage, the severity of the internal short circuit, or alternative distinguishing characteristics such as the specific electrode couples involved (e.g. Ca–An, Cu–Al). For example, Liu et al. [41] and Jia et al. [50] primarily define internal short circuit types based on voltage drop, classifying incidents as minor or major, or as soft or hard shorts.Liu et al. [41] further correlates these classifications with specific electrode pairs. Similarly, Duan et al. [51,52] focuses on voltage drop but places greater emphasis on categorization according to the electrode pairs involved. These two approaches should not be considered equivalent, as one emphasizes the origin of the short circuit while the other focuses on its consequences. Divergences can arise because similar internal short-circuit scenarios may lead to different outcomes depending on exogenous factors such as defect size, location, or separator properties.

In this section, we will focus on cells with graphite-based anodes, which have high electrical conductivity at the anode compared to cells using Lithium Titanate Oxide for example, and we will use the terms “soft internal short circuit” and “hard internal short circuit”.

A Soft internal short circuit represents the first stage in the evolution of internal short circuits, i.e. micro-short circuits (MSCs), which are caused by an intermittent or partial bridging of electrical potential between two points in a cell. Such short-circuits are referred to as the least dangerous types of short circuit, mainly resulting in a capacity loss. They are usually induced by contact between the copper current collector and the active material of the cathode and/or by contact between the active materials of the two electrodes (Anode-Cathode) [19,42,53–55] as represented in Fig. 1 (a). A Soft internal short circuit results in a higher resistance pathway expected to be at 100 mΩ or above for LIBs [19,38,46], leading to a lower current flow and lower heat generation. The threshold of 100 mΩ is sometimes used for differencing soft and hard internal short circuits but should be taken cautiously as this value can significantly varying depending on many parameters such as battery chemistry, size, geometry, state of charge, age and cause and position of the internal short circuit. The quoted 100 mΩ value should be considered as an order of magnitude and might for example seems low as it would lead to current as high as 40A for typical cell for 4.2V. Further experimental validation and discussion are necessary to refine this threshold for different cells. Notably, studies such as those conducted by Li et al. [56] have endeavored to elucidate this point, providing valuable insights into the characterizations of cell behavior and performance. However, there remains a need to enhance their findings by incorporating the previously mentioned conditions. Doing so would allow for a more accurate and relevant understanding of the systems in question, thereby contributing to the robustness of an established threshold.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Copper current collector and cathode active material internal short circuit
ce:section.ce:para: A Copper current collector-cathode active material internal short circuit leads to a low-intensity current since the cathode mixture is the worst electronic conductor among the cell components, as indicated in Table 1 . Supporting this hypothesis, Santhanagopalan et al. [40] studied Copper current collector-cathode active material internal short circuits on a 0.8 Ah and 1.8 Ah prismatic Li-ion cell with Mesocarbon Microbeads anode and cobalt-based cathode material (LiCoO2). Liu et al. [42] studied similar phenomenon on a 1 Ah pouch cell with Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2 cathode and graphite anode. Both studies agreed on a slight and “slow” voltage drop with a temperature increase considered insufficient (Fig. 1(b) and (c)) to initiate thermal runaway. The Joule heating power is measured at an order of 10−2 W [42]).Therefore, the Copper current collector-cathode active material short circuit is unlikely to induce a thermal runaway [19,40,42,53]. The temperature rise, which depends on battery type and capacity, should be nonetheless considered as the risk of thermal runaway still exist if the internal components are sensitive to moderate temperature rises.

Because the anode's thermal conductivity is lower than for copper current collector (Table 1), a short circuit will usually lead to localized heating. The rise in cell temperature resulting from an Anode-Cathode internal short circuit is higher than the one resulting from a Copper current collector-cathode active material internal short circuit. However, as the cathode has a low conductivity that limits the current flow and subsequently the Joule heating power, heat generation from Anode-Cathode generally remains limited [19,42]. Whether or not such internal short circuit can cause thermal runaway of a Li-ion cell remains a matter of debate. According to Santhanagopalan et al. [40], Orendorff et al. [53] and Liu et al. [42], the maximum local temperature reached in their studies on 18650 cells [40,53], 2032 coins cells [53] and 1Ah pouch Cells [42], respectively ranged between 60 °C and 75 °C (Fig. 1(b) and (c)). Such temperatures are not likely to induce thermal runaway, nor should they cause significant damage to common materials used. By contrast, studies of Fang et al. [54] highlighted that cell type could impact the maximal temperature reached. Inducing a Copper current collector-cathode active material internal short circuit on a Prismatic Li-ion cell of 1 Ah capacity leads to a maximum temperature of 110 °C. Such temperature values are not benign and are likely to cause degradation of some cell components (e.g., electrolyte, solid electrolyte interface) [4,5,22,30,44,57–60] and/or pore closing in the separator as the melting point of polyethene is approached (between 105 °C and 140 °C [61]).

An hard internal short circuit is created by a severe and immediate electrical fault with a low-resistance path that can lead to a sudden release of stored energy. This generates intense heat, most often cell failure and subsequently the phenomenon escalates to thermal runaway, potentially resulting in a fire. Similarly with an soft short circuit, an internal short circuit resistance threshold is sometime given in the literature with quoted values equal to or below 100 mΩ [19,38,46,49], therefore needing to be considered with caution. Short circuits involving the aluminum current collector of the cathode with either the copper current collector of the anode Aluminum and Copper current collectors or the active material of the anode Aluminum current collector - Anode [39] are commonly qualified as hard short circuit [19,38,41,49].

Aluminum and copper have excellent electrical and heat conductivities (Table 1). As a consequence, Aluminum and Copper current collectors short circuit, has the lowest internal short circuit resistance but, the high thermal conductivity of the copper current collector allows homogeneous heating throughout the cell (easier thermal dissipation), significantly limiting the onset of a thermal runaway event. An Aluminum and Copper current collectors internal short circuit tends to induce a fast rise in both generated power and heat but the heat dissipates quickly from the short circuit area [8,11]. In studies of Santhanagopalan et al. [40], Orendorff et al. [53] and Liu et al. [42], no thermal runaway event was observed although the cell temperature rises rapidly to roughly 100 °C at the contact of aluminum and copper, before progressively decreasing (Fig. 1(b) and (c)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aluminum current collector - anode internal short circuit
ce:section.ce:para: Two crucial factors contribute to the cell thermal runaway from an Aluminum current collector - Anode internal short circuit: (1) the aluminum and anode material have good electrical conductivities (Table 1), enabling short circuiting at high power levels and substantial/considerable heat generation; and (2) the anode's low heat conductivity induces an insufficient heat transfer and the development of hot spots on the anode side.

An Aluminum current collector - Anode short circuit is generally considered the worst and most dangerous situation of internal short circuit within most types of cells, resulting in a rapid and severe thermal runaway [19,42,53–55]. For instance, Santhanagopalan et al. [40], Liu et al. [42] and Fang et al. [54] studied batteries all exhibit a rapid drop of voltage to 0V with a similar increase of temperature to about 250 °C or well above (Fig. 1(b) and (c)). The values of the materials' electrical conductivities depend of its structural state, chemical composition [62,63], content of alloying elements [62–64], defect concentration [62,63] and external factors such as the magnetic [64] and electrical fields [65], temperature [63–65] and pressure [64,66–69]. Although Santhanagopalan et al. 's values in Table 1 do not consider most of these external factors, they can be used as means of comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal short circuits: causes and prevention
ce:section.ce:para: Internal short circuits represent a significant concern for cell manufacturers and electric car companies as they affect the performance of the batteries and safety of the user. They can occur at various stages in a battery's life, from manufacturing to its use and ageing (e.g., lithium dendrite formation). Following subsections (3.1 Production seek to provide an in-depth overview of the various causes of an internal short circuit and the methods currently used to observe and/or prevent them. It aims to lay the foundations for an advanced understanding of these phenomena and optimize the reliability of LIBs against internal short circuits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacturing defects: internal short circuit and thermal runaway estimations
ce:section.ce:para: In the context of battery production, it is essential that batteries undergo stringent quality control processes that adhere to standards such as the ISO 9001 to ensure the safety of production [70,71]. Despite regulatory safety requirements, which necessitate the implementation of quality controls defined by the manufacturer, a risk remains for defective batteries to reach market, leading to potential accidents. This highlights the need for continuous improvement in safety measures and testing protocols to further reduce risks.

According to EV Fire Safe [15], 2 % of LIBs fires between 2010 and January 2023 are due to manufacturing defects. These defects can indeed lead to internal short circuits and, ultimately, to thermal runaway. This number is probably underestimated as 49 % of the fire incidents are classified as "Unknown". In this context, studies have been carried out to quantitatively estimate the risk of a thermal runaway due to defect during the lifetime of a battery. Huang et al. [72] assumed that, in the study case of Tesla Model S, one vehicle in 10,000 would be defective. These estimates are broadly in line with Tesla's own estimates that one cell in a million will fail during its lifetime, i.e. the equivalent of 300 vehicles in 1 million (for a pack made up out of 300 cells) [7]. The "National Big Data Alliances of New Energy vehicles in China", predicts on their side that 0.9 to 1.2 vehicles in 10,000 would be defective [72]. From their own analysis in the matter, Loveridge et al. [59] have indicated that one cell in a million suffers a failure during manufacturing. Considering those figures, the probability of an internal short circuit due to production defects can be estimated at around [10−6 – 10−7] per cell.

Given the diversity of battery chemistries and form, further research is needed to comprehensively assess the failure rates and mechanisms of the various battery types currently put on the market. While this subsection provides valuable insights into the performance of cylindrical 18650 batteries and small prismatic cells, additional investigations are required to fully understand the reliability of larger prismatic and other battery designs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production faults causing internal short circuits
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, an internal short circuit could happen due to defects introduced during production. That is why quality control during production is essential, and defects must be detected at every step of the manufacturing process before the cells are put on the market [70]. To ensure the reliability of the final products, manufacturers implement various testing methods. For instance, Hi-pot (high potential) testing is commonly performed in the production line as a means to detect insulation failures or potential internal short circuits [73], thereby preventing defective cells from reaching the market. Production defects leading to internal short circuit and the means to detect and prevent them are detailed in this section and are summarized in Fig. 2 and Table 2 . A summary of the detection methods for production defects, along with their respective sensitivity, invasiveness, and commercial feasibility, is provided in Table S1 of the Supporting Information.

Slurry mixing is the first step in the manufacturing process of Li-ion battery cells. It consists of mixing the positive/negative active materials with the other constituents of the slurry used for making the battery electrodes [32,74,86]. The materials used for the manufacturing of the electrodes are generally powders of active compounds, such as graphite or Graphite/SiOx for the anode and lithiated metal oxides or phosphates for the cathode, as well as binders and conductive agents. These materials are mixed with a polymer in solution to form a homogeneous ink, called slurry, which is finally coated onto the current collector (coating step in 3.1.2.2 Coating) [32,74,86]. The mixing process generally involves several steps. First, the materials are accurately weighed according to a composition formula specific to each electrode type. Then, they are dry-mixed to achieve uniform particle distribution. Finally, a gel is added to create a homogeneous slurry that can be easily applied to the current collector [32,74,86]. The mixing process is critical for battery performance and life. If the materials are not mixed homogeneously, this can lead to a non-uniform reaction and induce lithium metal deposition and dendrite formation instead of lithium intercalation into the graphite potentially causing an internal short circuit. For example, in the case of NMC 622 slurry deposited on 75 μm thick electrodes, Bingbin Wu et al. [74] observed, as shown partially in Fig. 3 (a) , an accumulation of particles ranging from 45 to 100 μm using a grindometer. The average particle size should usually be less than 45 μm to avoid stress on the electrode and prevent particles pressing on the separator [74].

During this step, a thin layer of the anode or cathode slurry is applied to the surface of the current collectors (Al foil for cathode and Cu foil for anode) [59,74,77,87] using a specialized coating machine, such as a bar coater or roller coater [77,87]. Once applied, it is dried in a high-temperature oven to evaporate solvents and fix the binder. This drying process is crucial to ensure solid adhesion between the electrode film, the layer of active material and the current collector as well as the cohesion among the particles of active materials and conductive agents [76]. Mohanty et al. [77] studied NMC 532 electrodes to determine what types of defects might occur during the coating process. During their study, they observed defects such as the formation of blisters or agglomerates, recesses or holes, a substantial proportion of non-uniformed coating or the presence of contaminants (mostly metal particles). Among these identified flaws, only a metallic contaminant can directly lead to a short circuit, the others potentially acting as precursors in creating defects that induce short circuits in subsequent stages or during battery operation. Table S2 of supporting information provides further elements on faults observed during the coating process, their causes, their effects on the battery's final performance, and the techniques used to detect them. As an illustration, Loveridge et al. [59], using X-ray scanning and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) imaging techniques to examine the separator and electrodes (Fig. 3 (b)) of defective batteries of the Samsung Galaxy Note 7 revealed that the corners of the anode are more prone to a lack of coating. Folded areas are more affected by cracking of the coating layer. To mitigate the risk of delaminated zones set-up or, on the contrary, excess of particle agglomeration, Wu et al. [74] proposed to use a grindometer or to proceed to filtration of the slurry before the coating stage to remove too large particles.

The calendering step involves rolling the electrodes between two rollers to achieve a given porosity, a uniform thickness, enhanced adhesion and bottom line improved energy density. In the study conducted by Günther et al. [79], the authors classified the different types of calendering-induced electrode defects and aimed to understand their underpinning mechanisms. To this end, they used optical imaging techniques to characterize the defects, such as their size, shape and location. They showed that this step can actually cause defects in the electrodes, such as cracks, wrinkles and tears, pore closures, foreign particle introduction, non-uniform areas, air bubbles and deformations, which they grouped into three distinct sections, namely "geometrical", "structural" and "mechanical" defects reported in Table S3 of supporting information. Some of the defects for each section can be seen in the images of Fig. 3 (c1), (c2) and (c3). These defects can affect battery performance and lead to internal short circuits. Günther et al. [79] also studied the impact of these defects on subsequent production processes, such as battery notching and assembly. To do this, they set up a Domain Mapping Matrix using IWB GmbH's research production line as an example (Table S4 is available in supporting information). This study has shown that geometrical defects significantly impact the cell assembly stage, including stacking and electrolyte filling stages, as any obstacles or deviations in shape interfere with the free electrolyte flow and hinder the complete wetting of the electrodes. In this category and in general, the electrodes' waviness is considered the most destructive phenomenon and can considerably affect almost all processes of the cell assembly. On the other hand, the camber effect, corresponding to an elongation of the electrode along the coated area (also known as the banana effect or saber effect [79]), appears to be the most insignificant, affecting only the assembly stage. In the case of structural defects, defaults on electrode level can have consequences on electrolyte filling and electrical formation stages. Finally, mechanical defects would have a more significant impact on the cell assembly stage, which includes laser cutting and stacking. Foil tearing and electrode embrittlement could have consequences at almost any level of the manufacturing process; they are ranked second and third, among the most damaging events in battery manufacturing [79]. As a consequence, main risks associated with the calendering step are related to electrodes with defects such as cracks, pores, foreign particle introduction, and deformations [74,79,80,86]. It is interesting to point out that, according to the Günther's study, a particle pressed into the substrate or particle damage will not necessarily lead to an effect during the battery manufacturing processes. These faults can cause issues during the electrical formation step and when the battery is in use, as shown by studies by Tran et al. [88] and Meyer et al. [89].

After calendaring, the electrode is notched. This step consists of cutting a slot ("notch") on one side of the electrode foil where the active materials are not coated to reduce the stress induced by the compaction, facilitate efficient electrolyte interaction, and, most importantly, create "tabs" for making the electrical connection between each electrode and the cell electrical terminal [74]. Poor notching in a Li-ion battery occurs when one of the “tabs” inside the battery is damaged, twisted or bent, which can cause deformation of the battery structure. When this happens, there's a risk that the “tabs” come into contact with each other, causing an internal short circuit. For example, preliminary geometrical and structural defects on the electrodes, such as embrittlement, cracks, tears, or a local thickness and density fluctuation, could lead to compromised structural integrity during notching. Wu et al. [74] have shown the importance of a clean, controlled environment and precise cutting during this stage. Their studies have demonstrated the importance of the presence of particle "dust" and burrs on the edges of finished electrodes, which can induce an internal short circuit by perforating the separator. Fig. 3 (d) shows a SEM analysis, where, in a 12 μm-thick aluminium foil, the size of particles/burrs was twice the foil thickness, or even three times if the cut was made manually using scissors or a blunt blade.

Another common source of short circuit during cell assembly is the incorrect positioning of electrodes and separator: electrodes may be misaligned during assembly, resulting in direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes, creating a short circuit [74]. The stacking can be performed using several techniques: Single sheet stacking, Z-stacking, cylindrical winding or prismatic winding [74] (Fig. S1 in supporting information). Wu et al. mentioned that the Z-stacking process generates less stress and improves uniform stress distribution in the stacked cell while reducing possible environmental problems (such as particle infiltration) since the electrodes are in contact with the environment on only two sides instead of four, as two are wrapped by separators [74].

Once the electrodes are assembled into a stack (or jelly roll when made by winding), the electrode tabs are then connected to the electrical connection part by welding. The most suitable welding can also depend on the type of the manufactured cell. For example, Liu et al. [76] mentioned three welding technologies: Ultrasonic welding, Resistance welding and Laser welding.
-
Ultrasonic welding is frequently used in Li-ion cell assembly to join tabs or foils. It delivers accurate and localized energy, generating little heat-affected zones and allowing the joining of dissimilar materials. It is also low energy consumption. This welding technique is the most appropriate for thin materials.
-
Resistance Spot welding is a fast, cost-effective, low thermal input technique used for cylindrical and prismatic cells but has difficulty to join highly conductive and dissimilar materials.
-
Laser Welding is used for welding tabs or other components in Li-ion cell manufacturing. This technique offers the lowest contact resistance and highest tensile strength while offering high precision and speed. Nevertheless, the struggle to join dissimilar and highly reflective materials and its high cost make it difficult to use casually.

This step involves injecting a liquid electrolyte inside the cell as an ionic conductor enabling transport of Li ions. Several phenomena occurring during this phase can cause internal short circuits afterwards [84–86,90]. These may include.
-
Electrolyte contamination by impurities such as metal particles. Impurities can act as conductive bridges, creating a direct conduction path between the electrodes.
-
Excess electrolyte can enhance the availability of lithium-ions and solvents near the electrodes, promoting the creation of dendrites during use. These dendrites can cause an internal short circuit by going through the separator and creating a direct conduction path between the electrodes.
-
Lack of electrolyte (uneven electrolyte distribution) can induce the presence of dry spots with increased resistivity and non-uniform current distribution [84,85], promoting the creation of hotspots and dendrites.
-
Trapped air bubbles: During the electrolyte filling process, air bubbles can form and become trapped inside the cell. Trapped bubble or insufficient impregnation (or wetting) may cause an inhomogeneous Li-ion diffusion and, therefore, cause Li-plating that might ultimately lead to an internal short circuit.

To detect short circuits between a battery's cathode and anode as early as possible, as well as defects such as particles or voids in the separator, performing high-potential (Hi-pot) tests are generally applied (e.g. Dielectric Withstand Test; Insulation Resistance Test.).

Electric Formation is one of the most critical step in Li-ion battery manufacturing for multiple reasons including its future safety performance [91]. Battery cell formation means its electrochemical activation which involves repeatedly charging and discharging the cell (as well as other tests) to activate the materials and prepare them for use and ensure quality and repeatability of operation (maximum capacity and optimum performance) [91]. Stability of electrochemical reactions is also on focus during the process, since LIBs usually exceed electrochemical stability windows of most electrolytes. Nevertheless, if carried out incorrectly, it can damage the cell and even induce an internal short circuit.

Li plating may cause rapid performance fading and ageing of a battery cell (e.g., pore clogging, electrolyte degradation, …) [20,81,82,92–105]. Repeated and/or prolonged plating-promoting conditions may also lead to the formation of internal short circuits. Lithium can indeed grow into microscopic, branching structures called dendrites, which can develop into the electrolyte [20,21,82,103] and, if undetected, can puncture the separator, thus creating an internal short circuit [4,19,20,25,48,81,93,99,100,103–108] as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). This section investigates the conditions of occurrence and consequences of Li dendrites growth, as well as possible detection and tracking methods. One of the origin of lithium plating may be non-uniform electrode coating caused by manufacturing faults or abuse operating conditions [22,81–83] like high charging rates, use at low temperature or overcharging [20–22,82,100,105]. The origin of lithium plating can be divided into two distinct categories based on operating conditions relative to the battery's Safe Operating Range. For high charging rates, references [21,97,101,102,109] address plating occurring outside the Safe Operating Range, while studies [20,21,81,93,97,98,101–103,109] examine plating within the Safe Operating Range. Similarly, for low-temperature operation, plating outside the Safe Operating Range is discussed in Refs. [4,92,98,104,109], with references [4,81,82,93,101,103,109] covering cases within the Safe Operating Range. This classification highlights the importance of considering both extreme and nominal operating conditions when analysing lithium plating mechanisms.

The detection of lithium deposition and its dendritic morphology has been the subject of numerous studies, being a concern for extending the battery life, enhancing its performance, improving its safety, and preventing an internal short circuit. Table 3 reports the techniques used in the literature to characterize such phenomenon, including on specifically designed laboratory cells.

Kong et al. have, for example, studied the growth of the dendrites under different current densities from 10 to 110 mA/cm2 with optical spectroscopy, leveraging a see-through cell configuration incorporating lithium metal electrodes [106]. Under specific charging conditions, dendrites can eventually induce an internal short circuit by interconnecting the anode and cathode (Fig. 4 (c)). The dendrites grow much faster at high current density and change from a mossy to sharp morphology. In their case, a protrusion (indicated by the red arrows on Fig. 4 (c)) is formed on the surface of the negative electrode. The internal short circuit phenomenon occurred much faster at 110 mA/cm2 (0.5h) than at 10 mA/cm2 (19h) but in both cases without triggering thermal runaway [106].

Electrochemical analysis can be an excellent way to detect lithium plating and its dendritic growth during battery use. Uhlmann et al. [117] measured the cell voltage as a function of the state of charge at different pulses, Bai et al. measured the State Of Health as a function of the number of cycles at 1C [20], and Drees et al. [102] measured the CC discharge in function of the number of cycles at different C-rates all discovered graphically a knee point which can be associated with the formation of lithium plating (Fig. 5 ). Using various cell compositions and geometries with respectively half cells in an EL-Cell® ECC-Ref housing [117]; 800 mAh Sanyo's cylindrical lithium cobalt oxide UR14500P batteries [20] and an Electrode Equivalent Circuit Model (EECM) with Commercial 3-electrode cells in coin cell setup [102], they all observed that the appearance of a knee point is corelated with the presence of Li plating. This observation is crucial for Li plating detection and can be used as a criterion to determine that the battery should no longer be used to avoid dendrite formation.

As a whole, lithium plating, resulting from battery use outside of the Safe Operating Range, and manufacturing defects can all induce an internal short circuit, which can, over time, degrade the cell but, above all, cause a potentially serious safety problem. In this context, systems such as Battery Management Systems (BMS), thermal management, and charging rate control are designed to inhibit this phenomenon by controlling critical parameters like temperature and charging current. However, these measures alone are not sufficient to fully prevent the occurrence of lithium plating. As a result, additional strategies are being developed to mitigate its formation. These include modifying the electrode chemistry, such as the incorporation of additives that enhance performance [143–145], and targeting an optimal ion transfer number in the electrolyte to reduce the risk of plating [146–150]. Efforts are also focused on improving the anode through coatings that contain conductive agents, metals, or metal oxides, which can enhance lithium-ion diffusion [151,152]. Furthermore, innovations in anode design, such as reducing its particle size [151,153,154], its thickness and tortuosity [151,155] or create nanostructured carbon designs, are being explored [151,156]. Finally, there is ongoing research into functional separators that can further limit the formation of lithium plating by blocking the growth of lithium dendrite by mechanical barrier, ; redistributing the Li + ion flux when passing through the separator and or by favorizing the uniform deposition of Li + ions on the anode [157]. These separators provide an additional layer of protection, enhancing battery performance and reducing degradation.

Lithium plating is one of the underlaying cause of an internal short circuit. Distinguishing between lithium dendrite-induced and defect-induced internal short circuits during postmortem analysis remains a complex task. If thermal runaway has occurred, the damage often prevents direct identification of the internal short circuit origin, and the analysis must rely on pre-failure data such as cell age, usage conditions (e.g., low temperature, high C-rate charging [20–22,82,100,105]), and electrochemical signatures indicative of lithium plating. Conversely, if the cell remains are intact, which is expected to be the case for an dendrite induced internal short circuit, these data can be complemented by physical and chemical analysis. Lithium dendrite-induced internal short circuits may be identified through the presence of plated lithium with a characteristic metallic appearance on the separator and localized lithium enrichment detectable by ICP-OES [96,121], GD-OES [118,130–132], XPS [133] or ToF-SIMS with Auger electron spectroscopy [134]. Defect-induced internal short circuits are often linked to particle contamination, separator damage, or electrode misalignment, sometimes identifiable via high-resolution X-ray imaging. Ultimately, root cause determination requires a holistic and expert-driven approach, as evidence can be inconclusive or point to multiple potential failure mechanisms. It is also therefore essential to consider how an internal short circuit can be experimentally reproduced to provide critical insights into battery failure mechanisms and allow engineers to develop more reliable battery systems.

To simulate these defaults, many internal short circuit tests have been developed in the past, but depending on the parameter(s) studied, they can be judged as too severe (full nail penetration) or too soft (FISC test), leaving a large gap where most of the real-life short circuit failures happen. Recently, innovative internal short circuit tests have been presented, like the one from NASA using phase change material or the local internal heater method. Even if these methods have shown promising results, they can only be implemented by the cell manufacturer of engineered cells, limiting their use in normative frameworks or regulations. For clarity, we have classified the above techniques into three types: thermal, mechanical, and intentionally introduced defects. The experimental reproduction of internal short circuits faces significant cell-to-cell variability due to the diversity of triggering techniques and their inherent parametric sensitivities. Each method, whether thermal (internal resistance wire, external heat source), mechanical (nail penetration, blunt rod test, pinch torsion), or material-based (low-melting-point metals, NREL/NASA wax-based device, shape memory alloy, FISC) introduces distinct failure modes, impacted zones, and contact resistances. For instance, thermal methods add external energy, altering failure initiation, while mechanical tests depend heavily on indenter properties (hardness, geometry, speed). Specialized techniques like FISC require stringent environmental control to ensure consistency. The cell casing configuration (intact vs. pre-punctured) introduces additional variability in an internal short circuit test reproducibility, as casing deformation resistance differs substantially across cell formats. Pouch cells, with their flexible casings, offer minimal mechanical resistance to penetration, while hard prismatic or cylindrical steel casings require significantly greater force to breach, altering both short circuit initiation and propagation dynamics. This casing-dependent behavior creates fundamental inconsistencies when comparing internal short circuit results: tests on bare electrodes (no casing) or pre-pierced cells eliminate casing effects but deviate from real-world failure scenarios, whereas intact-cell tests introduce unpredictable deformation energy that varies with casing thickness and material properties. Consequently, the same trigger method (nail penetration, pinch torsion) may yield variation in failure characteristics between cell formats solely due to casing differences, further complicating cross-study comparisons. Moreover, even within the same test category with identical cells, variability arises from normative differences (e.g. UL vs. IEC standards dictating distinct nail shape, material or penetration speeds). As highlighted by Gao et al. [158], these variations such as subtle differences in penetration speed or indenter geometry can critically influence test outcomes, making achieving consistency across laboratories particularly challenging. The multitude of standards, each prescribing unique experimental conditions to fulfil a specific purpose, partly explains this variety, complicating internal short circuit tests reproducibility and comparative safety assessments across studies. The supporting information, Table S5, reports examples of standards and associated chapter(s) referencing experimental internal short circuit tests at the module or cell level while Table S6 give examples of Nail penetration test conditions in different norms.

The resistance wire heating test creates an internal short circuit in a battery by introducing resistance into the battery circuit, which causes a local increase in the battery's temperature by passing current [159–164] (e.g. Nichrome wire to produce 50 Watts of heating [159]). This rise in temperature causes degradation of the battery's internal components, such as electrolytes and separators (separator melting), which can lead to an internal short circuit [163,164] as illustrated in Fig. 7 (a). The resistance wire heating test assesses batteries' safety and thermal behaviour and provides insights into possible anomalies. Nevertheless, its destructive nature, limited real-world representations, need for careful insertion of the resistance wire during the manufacturing process, and lack of reproducibility cell to cell (single-point data) are all factors to consider when using it.

A localized heat source can cause a battery to short circuit internally by several physical mechanisms. Firstly, a heat source can increase the battery's temperature, which can cause thermal expansion or shrinkage of the materials, such as the separator [2,87,165,166] as represented in Fig. 7 (b) (ex: The use of an 0.4 kW cylindrical heater on a China Aviation Lithium Battery Co leaded to an increase of surface temperature to about 700 °C [165]). This can create unintended contact between battery parts that should not usually be in contact [159]. These contacts can cause an internal short circuit by providing an alternative path for electrons inside the battery, leading to rapid, uncontrolled discharge [2]. In addition, heat can also cause undesirable chemical reactions in the battery [2,165–168]. The materials used in batteries are often sensitive to temperature, and a temperature rise can alter their chemical properties and electrical behavior [4,5,22,30,44,57–60]. For example, overheating can degrade the separator, leading to inter-electrode short circuits or short circuits between the electrode and the battery casing [2]. In this case, there is no need to set up the heat source internally, making the process easier. However, it has the same drawbacks as using a resistance wire heating and even more as the impacted area of the internal short circuit is not controlled, perhaps leading to multiple hard and/or soft short circuits.

Lasers produce a highly concentrated and energetic beam of light that can damage battery materials and components. If a laser is directed at a battery, it can cause localized heating and melting of materials [113,169] (Fig. 7 (c)). In very specific conditions, interactions between the laser and cell provide an increasing exchange current density at the hotspot and its vicinity, drastically enhancing localized deposition of Li and its self-overgrowth [113]. However, laser impact destructive nature, limit its representativity and, most importantly, requires specialized equipment and expertise in the laser domain which make it a complex and resource-intensive technique.

When a battery is subjected to excessive physical force, the separator can crack or rupture, creating an internal short circuit [17,170,171]. A "pinch-torsion" test involves the simultaneous compression and torsion of a battery [17,170,171] as illustrated in Fig. 7 (d). This method is sometime used to assess batteries’ strength and safety. Compression and torsion of a battery lead to important deformation that affects internal components. Several failure mechanisms leading to internal short circuit have been identified: delamination of the coating materials [171], tearing of current collectors, kinking or creaking of layers, and local melting in the localized impacted zone [17]. A variety of setups can be used in a pinch-torsion test but most commons one are steel loading rods with a hemispherical end [170] or spheres [17,171]. Pinch torsion test mimics mechanical stress and torsion effects on a cell and is particularly appropriate if the purpose is to reproduce scenarios in which external mechanical forces may produce compression or pinching. It has the advantage to creates internal defects without systematically puncturing the cell casing and is adapted for all kind of cell geometry (cylindrical, prismatic, pouch). For example, Ren et al., using a 12,7 mm diameter hemispheres on dry cells constated that approximately 400 N of axial load with about 2.7° of rotation lead to an electrical short circuit versus 630N in pinch only. This resulted to non-central and smaller separator holes (0.05 mm vs 0.1 mm for pinch only) with a faster internal short circuit trigger. Limitations of pinch torsion includes the impossibility to precisely control the induced damage, or the internal component affected.

The blunt rod test consists in compressing a lithium-ion battery locally with a blunt cylindrical rod, usually made of stainless steel at a predetermined speed and force to simulate an impact. The rod is pressed against the surface of the battery with a certain amount of force until reaction or until it passes through the outer wall of the battery [41,172–174]. When the battery is abused, the internal layers of active and conductive materials may compress, deform, or break [18,41,42,173–176], forming internal short circuits (Fig. 7 (e)). Mechanical deformation induced by blunt rod test can lead to a minor (soft) or critical (hard) internal short circuit, the latter resulting in a temperature increase that melts the separator or the collector. Depending on the cell's State Of Charge and the component involved, this can lead to a non-hazardous situation or a thermal runaway. At medium and high State Of Charge, the cell's behavior depends on the predominant component involved. Liu et al. [41] determine that the separator melting (with mechanical pressing) leads mostly to a Hard internal short circuit, whereas the collector melting leads to a Soft internal short circuit. SEM characterization highlighted that the minor internal short circuit leads to less severe defects on the material and impacted surface, while the hard internal short circuit generates more serious failures, as holes are observed on electrodes and the separator. As for the pinch torsion, the blunt rod test is a mechanical abuse that allows to induce internal short circuit without perforating the cell casing but does not allow to select the internal component abused or control the exact location of the internal short circuit(s) making the outcome hardly reproducible.

As portrayed in Fig. 7 (f), penetration by nail causes an immediate short circuit, by puncturing the separator, and creating a direct electrical contact between the electrodes. [43]. It leads to rapid discharge and self-heating of the entire cell [159,177] that can end in thermal runaway (e.g. temperature recorded above 400° for a 3Ah cylindrical cell [159]). The nail penetration test is probably the method the most often used to produce internal short circuit but is not standardized. There is a large number of parameters reported in literature that influence the test outcome. Those parameters are detailed below and a recapitulative table with related publications can be find in supportive information (Table S7).
-
Nail speed: The literature describes nail penetration speeds ranging from 0.01 mm s-1 to 1000 cm s-1 [178–181]. Fast nail penetration tends to cause immediate and extensive damage to the separator and elements and induce more heat generation [179–181]. On the contrary, slow penetration can allow the battery's protective mechanisms to activate and in extreme cases very slow penetration can lead to progressive discharge. As a means of illustration, Mao et al. [180] investigating the impact of nail speed constated that the average maximum temperature difference recorded by the thermocouple (at peak temperature) is 247.6 ± 63.9 °C for 20 mm/s speed, 261.4 ± 9.4 °C for 30 mm/s speed and 327.5 ± 32.8 °C for 40 mm/s speed;
-
Nail material: Electronic conductive materials can establish an electrical connection between the battery's positive and negative electrodes and cause a rapid and direct short circuit within the battery [55,182–184]. On the contrary non electronic conductive material creates indirect short circuit by puncturing the separator and creating damages inside the cell [182,183]. An intermediate solution consists on using an electronically insulated nail (ceramic for example) with an electronic conductive tip. It has the ability to create a localized internal short circuit without creating a massive direct short circuit. In this aspect, as presented in Fig. 6
, Abaza et al. [182], demonstrated that a plastic nail did not produce a voltage drop at the battery terminals, even after 16 h, and did not lead to overheating. On the other hand, a copper nail generated a very sharp reduction in voltage, accompanied by a rapid rise in temperature. This figure also illustrates the lack of reproducibility that is often associated with this technique, as, same abuse conditions lead to a variety of results in term of end voltage or maximal temperature reached.

Another critical parameter to consider when performing a nail penetration test is the thermal characteristics of the nail's material. Indeed, materials with high heat capacities and thermal conductivities will quickly dissipate the heat generated at the point of the internal short circuit, decreasing the local temperature rise and influencing test outcome [185,186]. To limit this phenomenon, use of low thermal conductivity and heat capacity materials such as ceramic nails (with Ni tip) as seen in the norm IEC 62640-4 [187] or use of thermal and electrical isolating setup for the nail [186] are adapted.
-
Nail diameter: In the event of an internal short circuit, a duality is established. A larger nail diameter result in a smaller short circuit resistance and thus, a greater short circuit current, able to generate a higher total heat, favoring thermal runaway [178,185,188]. Depending on the test parameters used, the tendency can go reverse as the nail enables greater heat dissipation ending in lower temperature [179,184,185]. For example Wang and al [185], using an commercial 1 Ah Li(NixCoyMnz)O2 (NCM)/graphite pouch cells with tri-layer separator of polypropylene/polyethylene/polypropylene observed that the maximum temperature at nail site for a 3 mm nail, 5 mm and 8 mm tungsten-steel nails are respectively 130 °C,100 °C and 100 °C (with a faster heat dissipation than the 5 mm) without heat shield and about 120 °C, 115 °C and 100 °C with heat shield. The diameter of the nail should, therefore be chosen carefully depending on the test objective.
-
Tip shape: The shape of the tip of the nail used to puncture a LIB can affect the nature and severity of an internal short circuit event. A blunt (squared-off or flat tip) or rounded-tipped nail can generate a broader and less targeted puncture on the battery casing and separator, producing a more extensive and electrical channel between the battery's electrodes and raising the likelihood of a substantial internal short circuit more critical [178]. A nail with a pointed or needle-like tip, can create a small puncture point and a small defect in the separator. It potentially reduce the region involved in the internal short circuit that are reported, in specific conditions to lead to higher temperature [178].
-
Position of penetration: The position of the nail penetration has an influence on the internal current produced and the test result. Among the different penetration positions, the thermal runaway reaction is reported to be more severe when the battery is penetrated near the edges or the tabs. [179,189]. Two reason are proposed to explain these higher effects: higher electronic conductivity (as less active material with high resistance are involved) and poor heat dissipation (compared to the center were heat can be transferred to all directions) [179,189].
-
Direction of penetration: As nail penetration can be used on all cell geometries (cylindrical, prismatic and pouch), several penetration angle can be used. The vast majority of tests found in the literature are performed perpendicularly toward the electrodes. It is nonetheless possible to introduce the mail parallelly to the electrodes surface. The tests outcome can be totally different as, nail penetration perpendicularly to the electrodes (vertical) conducts to a voltage drop and important temperature increase whereas nail penetration conducted parallel to the electrode did not produce notable effects on voltage nor temperature [190]. One reason explaining this is the difference between the number of layers involved in the short circuit, the smaller this number, the more limited are the effects.
-
Depth of penetration: When penetration is perpendicular to the electrodes, penetration depth corresponds to the number of layers (electrodes and separator) the nail is inserted into. If the penetration is shallow, only a portion of the battery's separator may be damaged, thereby reducing the short circuit consequences. A moderate penetration depth can result in a more severe short circuit and a deep penetration frequently creates an immediate and severe short circuit, often resulting in thermal runaway, fire, or explosion [178–180,188]. The penetration depth must be controlled depending on the type of internal short circuit studied. For nail with insulated body and conductive tip, the depth of penetration corresponds to changing the position were the short circuit is induced.

In conclusion, the nail penetration test has advantages for safety evaluation as it is applicable to all kind of cells and reproduce to a certain extend the conditions of an internal short circuit. Some drawbacks are persisting, it is a destructive method, and its reproducibility should be improved. Also, as the nail need an entry point in the casing, this method results in contamination of the electrodes and electrolyte with oxygen from the air and water [159]. The hole in the casing also creates an exit path for thermal runaway gases that is not representative of a real internal short circuit scenario.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intentionally introduced defects: dedicated internal devices

ce:section.ce:para:
The first possibility to induce an internal short circuit by introducing defects is the use of low melting point metals. Due to their conductive nature, incorporating low-melting point metals into a battery creates an internal short circuit when they melt and come into contact with the battery's opposite electrodes [38] as represented in Fig. 7 (g). Some studies use metals such as Bismuth (Bi, Tmelt
≈
271.3 °C), Tin (Sn, Tmelt
≈
231.9 °C), Indium (In, Tmelt
≈
156.6 °C), or even Bi/Sn/In alloy (Tmelt
≈
60 °C) [38]. Orendorff et al. [38] demonstrated an internal short circuit triggering method by inserting a 1 mm2 Bi/Sn/In alloy piece into 2032 coin cells. For LiCO cells, the internal short circuit occurred between active materials (anode/cathode), while in NMC cells the short formed between current collectors (Al/Cu). The internal short circuit initiation temperature ranged consistently between 60 and 65 °C, with NMC (charged to 4.3V) exhibiting more severe thermal behavior, reaching temperatures up to 70 °C during failure. This type of test provides insights into possible defects and thermal resistance of the materials used in the battery. Nevertheless, the complexity of its practical implementation, its limited real-world representativity, and the difficulties to control impacted area of internal short circuits, which could lead to multiple short circuits (hard ones included), making it a less viable option for short circuit investigation.

This technique involves introducing a wax-based device inside the cell to trigger an internal short circuit (Fig. 7 (h)). Wax is not a heat-resistant element (Tmelt
≈
57 °C). By replacing (a part of) the separator between the anode and the cathode by the system and then heating the cell, the Wax melt. The copper disc under it permits a controlled conductive path between the battery's positive and negative plates, inducing a localized short circuit [47,191–194]. The wax-based NREL/NASA Device allows localized damage to specific regions of lithium-ion batteries to be simulated in a controlled way, enabling specific studies to be carried out on the types of internal short circuits. However, its destructive nature and, most importantly, the need to disassemble, adapt and reassemble LIBs make it a complex and resource-intensive method.

A particular technique implemented in the studies of Zhang et al. [39,195] is the use of a shape memory alloy (SMA), which significantly differs from foreign particle introduction or more traditional nail penetration test. As portrayed in Fig. 7 (i), this shape-memory alloy has the specificity of rising at high temperatures (heat-treated Ni-Ti alloy with an internal short circuit trigger at 70 °C inside the cell), causing the separator to puncture and induce an internal short circuit [39,195]. In simulated aluminum-anode internal short circuit tests using fabricated NCM prismatic pouch cells, an internal short circuit initiation occurred between 64.2 and 72.3 °C, followed by rapid thermal runaway reaching peak surface temperatures of 385.8–392.7 °C at the cell center within approximately 20 s [39]. Given its compact size, high power density, and corrosion resistance, the Shape Memory Alloy won't influence the battery's performance or electrochemical system during the test. However, its lack of representativeness, SMA cost, and, most importantly, its integration challenge and reliability of SMA perforation power, which are likely variable depending on cell size, geometry, and material, are parameters to consider for its use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle incorporation and pressing/Forced internal short circuit test (FISC test)
ce:section.ce:para: Incorporating foreign particles into a battery can cause an internal short circuit by disrupting the battery structure or creating an alternate path for the flow of electric current [11,39–41,54,195–198]. When carrying out this test, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (j), a nickel particle is introduced between two layers of the battery [11,40,196,198,199] (but it can be also Copper, Iron, Steel and many others [196,200,201]), and the battery is crushed (usually by a press). During this process, particles can be electrochemically dissolved, diffuse and replate, creating a conductive path between the positive and negative electrodes [11,48,82,196,200,201] or perforating the separator [11,40,48,196,200] inducing an internal short circuit. Performing this type of test allows individual studies of each type of internal short circuit. Again, it is quite complex, requiring the cell to be disassembled and then reassembled without impacting it or introducing other foreign particles [197–199] and can lead to irreversible damage on the cell after test.

In our exploration of internal short circuits, we have identified a range of techniques used to reproduce these phenomena, including thermal methods, mechanical stress applications, and intentionally introduced defects with internal devices. Each technique provides distinct insights into the underlying mechanisms and failure modes associated with internal short circuits. Table 4 provides an overview of main internal short circuit testing methods discussed, including technique key characteristics, advantages and known limitations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Initial perspectives on computational and AI-based approaches to internal short circuit investigation
ce:section.ce:para: Alongside experimental efforts, the rise of the digital era has profoundly transformed the landscape of battery research. Advanced modeling techniques and the advent of artificial intelligence now play an increasingly vital role, offering alternative pathways to bridge persistent knowledge gaps and to better understand complex failure mechanisms. Predictive modeling for Internal Short Circuits has evolved significantly, driven by the need to enhance battery safety and reliability. Multiscale models integrating electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical dynamics have been developed to simulate internal short circuits initiations and evolutions under various conditions [40,41,202–207]. These models often specialize in specific phenomena like lithium plating or mechanical stress concentrations, offering targeted insights rather than encompassing all potential internal short circuit causes. Complementing these, physics-based models provide detailed understanding of local stress distributions and thermal runaway scenarios, enhancing the ability to predict internal short circuit risks. Most recently, machine learning models have emerged as powerful tools for early detection, analyzing impedance patterns, temperature signatures, and voltage anomalies to identify subtle precursors such as voltage drops and abnormal capacity fade [208–212]. Techniques like neural networks, Random Forest [208], Support Vector Machine [209]; Support Vector Regression [210], GRU-VAE [211], KPCA [212] excel at extracting complex patterns from noisy data, enabling real-time monitoring and early warning systems. However, challenges remain in acquiring realistic failure datasets, linking anomalies to physicochemical phenomena, and generalizing models across diverse battery chemistries and operational conditions. These advancements hold promise for improving battery design, safety protocols, and lifecycle management, paving the way for proactive mitigation strategies like digital twins and dynamic risk prediction.

Internal short circuits in lithium-ion batteries represent one of the most problematic failure mechanisms, because of their ability to cause catastrophic events such as thermal runaway without being detected by conventional BMS. This review has explored the multidimensional aspects of internal short circuits, presenting a thorough analysis of their types, origins, detection methods, and experimental reproduction techniques.

The internal short circuits were first categorized based on their physical characteristics and consequences, ranging from soft internal short circuits with minimal heat generation and capacity loss to hard internal short circuits that rapidly lead to high temperature and thermal runaway. The review highlighted how internal short circuits, starting as minor issues can evolve over time into a more severe failure characterized by heat and pressure buildup, or electrolyte and SEI decompositions. The transition from localized damage to large-scale failure emphasizes the importance of early detection and intervention.

The detection of internal short circuits remains a challenge, particularly in their early stages. While traditional methods such as scanning electron microscopy, optical microscopy, EDS map, voltage monitoring and impedance spectroscopy provide valuable indicators of the cause, origin of the internal short circuit, and the battery health, techniques that offer promise in identifying internal short circuits before they escalate are still under research. A combination of electrochemical and physical detection methods seems to be crucial to ensure a comprehensive internal short circuit detection across different stages of battery use. Finally, this review examined various experimental techniques used for reproducing internal short circuits in a controlled laboratory environment. Methods such as thermal, mechanical abuse or intentionally incorporated defects have been used to simulate the conditions that lead to internal short circuits. These experiments are essential for understanding the detailed mechanisms of an internal short circuit formation and progression, ultimately enabling the development of more robust and safer battery designs. However, creating internal short circuits that reproduce real-world failures remains challenging, as the complex interactions between battery components are difficult to replicate fully. Alongside experimental efforts, the digital era has reshaped battery research, with modeling and AI now central to understanding internal short circuits. Multiscale and physics-based models simulate failure mechanisms under varying conditions, while machine learning enables early detection through pattern recognition. Techniques like Random Forest and Support Vector Machine help monitor anomalies in real time. Despite progress, challenges remain in data availability and model generalization across chemistries and conditions.

In conclusion, preventing internal short circuits in lithium-ion batteries requires a multidimensional approach incorporating materials engineering, cell design, and safety management improvements. Advances in separator technology, thermal management systems, and early detection techniques offer hope for reducing an internal short circuit occurrence leading to thermal runaway. Moreover, continued research into the electrochemical, thermal and mechanical processes underlying an internal short circuit formation will be essential for developing safer (more thermal runaway resilient) and more reliable LIBs for various applications, from electric vehicles to portable electronics. This review emphasizes that a deeper understanding of the conditions leading to internal short circuits, alongside better detection and prevention strategies, will be crucial for developing of high-performance and safe lithium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the author(s) used Grammarly and GPT model 4o in order to improve language and readability. After using these tools, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.
